title: Git & GitHub Workflow
description: Version control basics every developer must master. Branches, PRs, conflicts.

questions.q: What is Git?
options: A distributed version control system for tracking changes in code over time
questions.explanation: Git tracks every change you make, lets you undo, branch, collaborate, and recover work that would otherwise be lost.

questions.q: What's the DIFFERENCE between Git and GitHub?
options: Git is the version control tool that runs locally; GitHub is a cloud service that hosts Git repositories
questions.explanation: Other Git hosts exist: GitLab, Bitbucket, Codeberg. Git itself is open source and works without any cloud service.

questions.q: Why is Git called 'distributed'?
options: Every developer has a full copy of the entire repository history, not just the current files
It works on distributed cloud servers
It distributes code automatically to users
Multiple people edit the same file simultaneously
questions.explanation: You can commit, branch, view history, and revert entirely offline. The 'remote' is just one more copy of the repo.

questions.q: Who created Git, and why?
options: Linus Torvalds in 2005 to manage Linux kernel development after a licensing dispute with the previous tool
questions.explanation: Git was built by the creator of Linux himself, specifically to handle Linux's massive distributed development. Built in 2 weeks.

questions.q: What's the BIGGEST reason every developer needs to know Git?
options: It's the universal standard for collaborating on code in essentially every modern company and open-source project
questions.explanation: Not knowing Git in 2026 is like not knowing email. It's a baseline professional skill.

questions.q: What is a 'repository' (repo) in Git?
options: A folder containing your project + a hidden .git folder that stores its complete version history
questions.explanation: The .git folder IS the version control. Delete it and you have a regular folder. Don't delete it.

questions.q: Which command initializes Git tracking in a folder?
questions.explanation: Run once in a new project folder. Creates the .git directory and begins tracking. Adding existing files requires git add.

questions.q: Which command downloads an existing remote repository to your computer?
questions.explanation: git clone creates a local copy of a remote repo, sets up the 'origin' remote, and checks out the default branch.

questions.q: What's inside the .git folder?
options: All commits, branches, refs, configs — the complete history of the repository
questions.explanation: Never edit .git manually. Use Git commands. Corrupting .git can lose your history.

questions.q: How do you check if a folder is a Git repo?
options: Run `git status` — it errors if not a repo, or shows status if it is
questions.explanation: git status is the most-used Git command. It tells you what's changed, staged, and the current branch.

questions.q: What is the 'staging area' (index) in Git?
options: A holding zone where you assemble exactly which changes go into your next commit
questions.explanation: Staging lets you commit only the changes you want, even if you've edited many files. Power feature of Git.

questions.q: Which command stages a file for the next commit?
questions.explanation: git add . stages everything in the current directory. git add -p (patch) stages by hunk for precision.

questions.q: Which command creates a commit from staged changes?
questions.explanation: -m provides the message inline. Without -m, Git opens your editor for a longer message.

questions.q: What's a GOOD commit message?
options: Concise present-tense summary of what changed and why: 'Add user login form' or 'Fix off-by-one in pagination'
questions.explanation: Future you (and your team) will thank you. Conventional Commits (feat:, fix:, docs:) is a popular formal standard.

questions.q: What does `git commit -am 'msg'` do?
options: Stages all TRACKED modified files AND commits them in one step (skips git add for tracked files)
Forces a commit even if nothing changed
questions.explanation: Shortcut for routine commits. Note: -a only stages modifications to tracked files; new files still need git add.

questions.q: Which command shows the COMMIT HISTORY?
questions.explanation: git log --oneline gives a compact view. git log --graph --all shows branch topology visually.

questions.q: Which command shows UNSTAGED changes (what you've edited but not git added)?
questions.explanation: git diff (unstaged), git diff --staged (staged but not committed), git diff HEAD (everything since last commit).

questions.q: Which command shows what files have been modified, staged, or untracked?
questions.explanation: Your most-used Git command. Run it before every commit to know exactly what you're about to commit.

questions.q: How do you see what changed in a SPECIFIC commit?
questions.explanation: Shows the commit message, author, date, and full diff. The hash can be the first 7+ characters.

questions.q: What is a 'commit hash' (SHA)?
options: A unique fingerprint (40-char hex) identifying each commit — derived from the commit's content
questions.explanation: The same content always produces the same hash. Hashes are how Git tracks commits internally.

questions.q: What is a Git BRANCH?
options: A movable pointer to a commit, allowing parallel lines of development
questions.explanation: Branches are lightweight in Git — just a pointer. Create them freely. They're the foundation of collaborative workflows.

questions.q: What's the default branch name in most modern repos?
questions.explanation: GitHub, GitLab, and most communities adopted 'main' as the default starting in 2020.

questions.q: Which command creates AND switches to a new branch?
options: git checkout -b feature-x  (or modern: git switch -c feature-x)
questions.explanation: The newer git switch command (introduced 2019) is clearer than the overloaded git checkout. Both still work.

questions.q: Which command switches to an EXISTING branch?
options: git checkout branch-name  (or modern: git switch branch-name)
questions.explanation: git switch only does branch switching (safer). git checkout also handles file operations (older, broader command).

questions.q: Which command lists all branches?
options: git branch  (add -a for remote branches too)
questions.explanation: The current branch is marked with * . -v shows the latest commit on each branch.

questions.q: What does merging do in Git?
options: Combines changes from one branch into another, creating a merge commit (or fast-forwarding)
Copies files to a new folder
questions.explanation: Merging is the standard way to integrate completed work from a feature branch back into main.

questions.q: Which command merges another branch INTO your current branch?
questions.explanation: First, git switch main. Then git merge feature-x to bring feature-x's changes into main.

questions.q: What is a 'fast-forward' merge?
options: When the target branch hasn't diverged, Git just moves the pointer forward — no merge commit needed
A merge that runs faster than normal
questions.explanation: Fast-forward keeps history linear. Use --no-ff to force a merge commit if your team prefers explicit merges.

questions.q: What is a 'merge conflict'?
options: When the same lines have different changes on each branch — Git can't auto-decide which to keep
When a branch refuses to merge
questions.explanation: Conflicts are normal in collaborative work. Git marks the conflicting sections in files with <<<<<<< for you to resolve manually.

questions.q: How do you RESOLVE a merge conflict?
options: Edit the file to keep the desired changes, remove conflict markers, git add the file, then git commit
Wait for it to resolve itself
questions.explanation: Use a merge tool (VS Code has a great built-in one) to make this less error-prone than editing manually.

questions.q: What does `git rebase main` do on a feature branch?
options: Replays your feature branch's commits on top of the latest main, creating a linear history
questions.explanation: Rebase is an alternative to merge for integrating changes. Cleaner history, but rewrites commit hashes — never rebase shared branches.

questions.q: What's the GOLDEN RULE of rebasing?
options: Never rebase branches that other people are working on — it rewrites history and breaks their copies
questions.explanation: Rebase = rewrite. Rewriting public history forces collaborators to do confusing recovery. Rebase local work only.

questions.q: What is INTERACTIVE rebase used for?
options: Squashing, reordering, editing, or dropping commits before pushing to clean up your history
questions.explanation: git rebase -i HEAD~5 lets you rewrite the last 5 commits. Great for tidying up a messy feature branch before opening a PR.

questions.q: What's the DIFFERENCE between merge and rebase?
options: Merge preserves true history with merge commits; rebase rewrites for a linear history
Merge is older, rebase is newer
Merge is for solo work, rebase for teams
questions.explanation: Choice of style. Many teams: merge for shared branches (preserve true history), rebase for personal branches (clean up before PR).

questions.q: If a rebase goes wrong, how do you ABORT it?
questions.explanation: --abort returns you to the state before the rebase started. Use it freely if a rebase gets confusing mid-way.

questions.q: What is a Git 'remote'?
options: A named reference to a copy of the repository hosted elsewhere (commonly named 'origin')
questions.explanation: When you clone, the source is automatically named 'origin'. You can add more remotes (e.g., 'upstream' for forks).

questions.q: Which command shows all configured remotes?
questions.explanation: -v shows the URLs. Each remote has separate fetch and push URLs (usually the same).

questions.q: Which command uploads your local commits to the remote?
questions.explanation: First push of a new branch: git push -u origin branch-name. Sets the upstream so future pushes/pulls use that remote/branch.

questions.q: Which command downloads new commits from the remote (but doesn't merge them)?
questions.explanation: fetch updates your local copy of the remote's branches. You can review before merging. Safer than pull.

questions.q: What does `git pull` do?
options: git fetch + git merge — downloads remote changes and merges them into the current branch
Pulls a file from the server
Pulls a branch out of existence
Pulls all commits to a new folder
questions.explanation: Convenient but can surprise you with merge commits. `git pull --rebase` keeps history linear.

questions.q: How do you UNSTAGE a file you've already git added?
options: git restore --staged file.txt  (or older: git reset HEAD file.txt)
questions.explanation: git restore (introduced 2019) is the modern dedicated command for undoing changes to the working dir or index.

questions.q: How do you DISCARD unstaged changes to a file?
options: git restore file.txt  (older: git checkout -- file.txt)
questions.explanation: WARNING: discarded changes cannot be recovered. Make sure you really don't want them.

questions.q: How do you AMEND the last commit (change message or add forgotten changes)?
questions.explanation: Adds your currently-staged changes to the previous commit and lets you edit the message. Only amend commits not yet pushed.

questions.q: What's the DIFFERENCE between `git revert` and `git reset`?
options: Revert creates a NEW commit that undoes a previous one (safe for shared); reset moves the branch pointer (rewrites history)
questions.explanation: Revert = additive, safe for public history. Reset = destructive, for private history only. Choose carefully.

questions.q: How do you UNDO the last commit but keep changes staged?
questions.explanation: --soft keeps changes staged. --mixed (default) unstages but keeps changes. --hard discards everything (be careful).

questions.q: What does `git stash` do?
options: Temporarily saves your uncommitted changes and reverts the working directory to clean
questions.explanation: Stash is for the 'I need to switch branches NOW but I'm not ready to commit' moment. Saves work without polluting history.

questions.q: How do you RESTORE stashed changes?
options: git stash pop  (or git stash apply to keep the stash)
questions.explanation: pop applies and removes the stash. apply keeps it in case you need to apply it elsewhere too.

questions.q: How do you LIST all your stashes?
questions.explanation: Stashes are stacked. The newest is stash@{0}. You can pop/apply specific ones: git stash pop stash@{2}.

questions.q: What's a GOOD use case for stash?
options: Mid-work, you need to quickly switch branches to fix something urgent
questions.explanation: Stash is for temporary saves. For longer-lived WIP, create a 'wip' branch with proper commits instead.

questions.q: What happens to stashes when you delete the .git folder?
options: They are lost permanently — stashes are stored locally only
questions.explanation: Stashes never push to remote. Treat them as ephemeral; use commits/branches for anything important.

questions.q: What is GitHub?
options: A cloud platform that hosts Git repositories with collaboration tools (PRs, issues, actions, wikis)
questions.explanation: GitHub is owned by Microsoft (since 2018). Other Git hosts: GitLab, Bitbucket, SourceHut, Codeberg.

questions.q: What's a 'fork' on GitHub?
options: Your own copy of someone else's repository, used to contribute or experiment without affecting the original
questions.explanation: Forks are the open-source contribution model: fork → branch → change → push → PR back to the original.

questions.q: What's a 'pull request' (PR)?
options: A proposal to merge changes from one branch (often a fork) into another, with review and discussion
A request to pull files locally
questions.explanation: PRs are the heart of GitHub collaboration. They enable code review, CI checks, and structured discussion.

questions.q: What's an 'issue' on GitHub?
options: A trackable record of a bug, feature request, task, or discussion topic
questions.explanation: Issues + Projects make GitHub a lightweight project management tool. Link PRs to issues with 'Fixes #123' in the description.

questions.q: What is a README.md file?
options: The primary documentation file shown on the repo's home page — describes the project, install steps, usage
A backup of all other files
questions.explanation: A great README is the difference between a useful repo and an abandoned one. Cover: what, why, install, usage, license.

questions.q: What's a TYPICAL PR workflow?
options: Branch → commit → push → open PR → review → address feedback → merge
Edit files on the GitHub web
questions.explanation: This branch-and-PR flow is the industry standard. Main is protected; all changes go through PRs and review.

questions.q: Why do teams REVIEW pull requests?
options: Catch bugs, share knowledge, maintain quality, and discuss design decisions before code merges
Reviews are optional and rarely done
questions.explanation: Code review is one of the highest-leverage activities in software engineering. Multiplies team knowledge AND code quality.

questions.q: What's a SMALL PR?
options: A focused change of <300 lines that's easy to review thoroughly
A PR for a small feature
A PR by a junior developer
questions.explanation: Big PRs get rubber-stamped because they're too painful to review. Small focused PRs get real review and ship faster.

questions.q: What is a 'draft PR'?
options: A PR marked as work-in-progress — visible for early feedback but not ready to merge
questions.explanation: Use draft PRs for early visibility, CI testing on your branch, or to discuss approach before finishing.

questions.q: What does 'squash and merge' do on GitHub?
options: Combines all the PR's commits into one commit when merging to main, keeping history clean
questions.explanation: Popular default for feature PRs. Keeps main's history clean and reverts easy. The full PR history is preserved on the branch.

questions.q: What is .gitignore?
options: A file listing patterns of files Git should NOT track (node_modules, .env, build outputs)
questions.explanation: Critical for security and repo size. Without it, you'll commit secrets and 200MB of node_modules. Add it FIRST in every project.

questions.q: What should ALWAYS be in .gitignore?
options: Secrets/.env, node_modules/, build/dist folders, OS files (.DS_Store), editor files
questions.explanation: Anything that's: generated, environment-specific, secret, or huge belongs in .gitignore. Source code does not.

questions.q: What happens if you commit a secret API key by accident?
options: It's in your repo HISTORY forever (even if you delete it) — rotate the key immediately
Just delete it in the next commit
questions.explanation: GitHub auto-scans for leaked secrets. ROTATE the key first; then use git filter-repo to scrub history (advanced) — but always rotate.

questions.q: How do you UNTRACK a file that's already committed?
options: git rm --cached file.txt — removes from Git's tracking but keeps the file locally
questions.explanation: .gitignore only ignores untracked files. Already-tracked files need git rm --cached first.

questions.q: Which command sets your global Git username?
questions.explanation: Also set user.email. These appear in your commits. Set both before your first commit on a new machine.

questions.q: What's the RECOMMENDED way to authenticate to GitHub from Git CLI?
options: SSH keys, or HTTPS with a Personal Access Token (passwords are no longer accepted)
questions.explanation: GitHub deprecated password auth in 2021. SSH keys are easiest for daily use; PATs for scripts and CI.

questions.q: What is a Personal Access Token (PAT)?
options: A revocable token that replaces your password for Git/API authentication
questions.explanation: Fine-grained PATs (newer) let you scope a token to specific repos and permissions — safer than classic full-access PATs.

questions.q: How do you set up SSH authentication for GitHub?
options: Generate an SSH key (ssh-keygen), upload the .pub to GitHub settings, then clone with git@github.com URLs
questions.explanation: One-time setup, lifetime convenience. No password prompts on every push.

questions.q: What's GitHub CLI (gh)?
options: An official command-line tool for GitHub-specific operations like creating PRs, managing issues, viewing actions
questions.explanation: `gh pr create`, `gh issue list`, `gh repo clone`. Streamlines GitHub workflows from the terminal.

questions.q: What is two-factor authentication (2FA) on GitHub?
options: A required security layer using a second device/app (TOTP, passkey) in addition to password
questions.explanation: GitHub requires 2FA for all accounts that contribute code to public repos. Use a passkey or authenticator app, not SMS.

questions.q: What is a Git TAG?
options: A permanent named pointer to a specific commit, usually used to mark releases (v1.0, v2.3.1)
questions.explanation: Unlike branches, tags don't move. Use them to mark important commits like version releases.

questions.q: How do you create an annotated tag?
questions.explanation: Annotated tags (-a) include author, date, and message. Lightweight tags (no -a) are just a name. Annotated for releases.

questions.q: How do you push tags to the remote?
options: git push origin v1.0  (or git push --tags for all)
questions.explanation: Tags don't push by default with regular git push. You must explicitly push them.

questions.q: What is a GitHub RELEASE?
options: A formal package created from a tag, with release notes, downloadable assets, and a dedicated page
questions.explanation: Releases turn tags into shareable artifacts with notes and downloads. Common for libraries, apps, and installers.

questions.q: What is Semantic Versioning (SemVer)?
options: A versioning convention: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH where each part has specific meaning about breaking changes, features, fixes
questions.explanation: Major: breaking changes. Minor: new features (backward-compatible). Patch: bug fixes. Industry standard for libraries.

questions.q: What is 'GitHub Flow'?
options: A simple workflow: branch from main → commit → open PR → review → merge to main → deploy
questions.explanation: Lightweight, well-suited for continuous deployment. Most modern teams use this or a close variant.

questions.q: What is 'Git Flow' (the older alternative)?
options: A more structured workflow with develop, release, and hotfix branches alongside main
questions.explanation: Useful for projects with scheduled releases (e.g., desktop apps). Overkill for most web teams using continuous deployment.

questions.q: Why use BRANCH PROTECTION on main?
options: Prevents direct pushes, requires PR review, enforces CI checks — protects production code from mistakes
questions.explanation: Branch protection rules turn 'we should review code' into 'we cannot skip review'. Set them up on every important branch.

questions.q: What is a CODEOWNERS file?
options: A file mapping paths to GitHub users/teams who must review changes in those paths
questions.explanation: When a PR touches files matched in CODEOWNERS, the matching owners are auto-requested for review. Great for accountability.

questions.q: What's the BEST way to handle a botched force push that overwrote teammates' work?
options: Use the reflog (git reflog) to find the lost commit, then reset/force-push to restore it — talk to your team
Push more force pushes to fix it
questions.explanation: git reflog is your time machine — it records every change to HEAD. Most 'lost' commits can be recovered for 90 days.

questions.q: What is GitHub Actions?
options: A built-in CI/CD platform for running automated workflows (tests, builds, deployments) on Git events
questions.explanation: Free for public repos and generous free tier for private repos. The standard for CI in GitHub-hosted projects.

questions.q: Where do GitHub Actions workflows live?
questions.explanation: Workflows are YAML files version-controlled with your code. Triggers: push, pull_request, schedule, workflow_dispatch, etc.

questions.q: What's a TYPICAL CI workflow on every PR?
options: Install dependencies → run linter → run tests → build → report status on the PR
questions.explanation: CI catches bugs before review. Failing CI on a PR usually blocks merge via branch protection rules.

questions.q: What are GitHub Secrets used for?
options: Storing sensitive values (API keys, tokens) that Actions can access without committing them to the repo
questions.explanation: Secrets are env vars available only at runtime. Never log them. GitHub auto-redacts known secrets from workflow logs.

questions.q: What's an Action 'marketplace' action?
options: A reusable workflow step published by the community — use with `uses: actions/checkout@v4`
questions.explanation: Common actions: actions/checkout, setup-node, cache, upload-artifact. Audit third-party actions before using — they run YOUR code.

questions.q: How do you contribute to an open-source project on GitHub?
options: Fork → clone → branch → commit → push → open PR to the original repo
Edit on the GitHub web interface only
questions.explanation: This fork-and-PR flow is universal across virtually every OSS project hosted on GitHub.

questions.q: What should you check BEFORE opening a PR to an open-source project?
options: CONTRIBUTING.md, existing issues, project style guide, and ideally open an issue first to discuss
questions.explanation: Maintainers' time is precious. A well-researched PR with prior discussion is much more likely to be merged than a surprise drive-by.

questions.q: What's a 'good first issue' label?
options: Issues maintainers have flagged as beginner-friendly, with enough context for newcomers to tackle
The very first issue ever filed
An issue from a senior dev
questions.explanation: The best way to start contributing. Many projects also use 'help wanted', 'documentation', 'beginner-friendly'.

questions.q: What's a LICENSE file?
options: A file declaring the legal terms under which others can use, modify, and distribute your code
questions.explanation: Without a license, code is 'all rights reserved' by default — nobody can legally use it. Add MIT/Apache 2.0/etc. early.

questions.q: What is a Code of Conduct?
options: A document setting community behavior expectations and reporting procedures for a project
questions.explanation: Welcoming, professional communities ship more and last longer. CoCs (often based on the Contributor Covenant) set the tone.

questions.q: Why is COMMITTING OFTEN better than rare giant commits?
options: Small commits are easier to review, debug (git bisect), revert, and explain in messages
Git runs faster with many commits
questions.explanation: Each commit should be one logical change. 'WIP' commits are fine on a feature branch — squash before merging.

questions.q: What's an ANTI-PATTERN with Git?
options: Force-pushing to shared branches without telling collaborators
questions.explanation: Force pushes rewrite history. On shared branches, they overwrite others' work. Coordinate carefully if absolutely needed.

questions.q: When should you NEVER commit something?
options: When it contains secrets, credentials, large binary files, or sensitive personal data
questions.explanation: Use .gitignore + tools like git-secrets to prevent. For large files, use Git LFS or a separate artifact store.

questions.q: What's a HEALTHY commit message pattern?
options: First line: short summary (<72 chars). Blank line. Then optional body explaining WHY (not WHAT — diff shows what)
questions.explanation: Conventional Commits adds machine-readable prefixes (feat:, fix:, docs:). Pairs well with auto-changelogs and semver bumps.

questions.q: What's the BEST way to recover lost work in Git?
options: git reflog — shows every change to HEAD, even from operations you thought destroyed the commit
questions.explanation: Git rarely loses data permanently. reflog is your time machine for the last ~90 days, even after destructive operations.

questions.q: What is `git bisect`?
options: A binary search through commit history to find which commit introduced a bug
questions.explanation: bisect can pinpoint the bad commit in a 1000-commit history with ~10 tests. Genuinely magical when debugging regressions.

questions.q: What is `git cherry-pick`?
options: Applies a specific commit from another branch onto the current branch
questions.explanation: Useful for backporting fixes to a stable branch without merging the entire feature branch.

questions.q: What is a Git 'hook'?
options: A script that runs automatically on Git events (pre-commit, post-merge, pre-push) to enforce rules or run tasks
questions.explanation: Local hooks live in .git/hooks. Tools like Husky make them version-controlled and team-shared (e.g., run linters pre-commit).

questions.q: Which is a GOOD habit to develop with Git?
options: Pull before you start work, commit often, push regularly, write meaningful messages
questions.explanation: Smooth Git habits prevent conflicts, lose less work, and make collaboration painless. Practice them daily.

questions.q: What's the BEST way to keep learning Git?
options: Use it daily on real projects, read the Pro Git book (free), and don't fear breaking things — Git is hard to truly break
questions.explanation: The free Pro Git book is the canonical reference. Confidence comes from doing — Git's safety net (reflog, branches) is huge.
